query: clinical manifestations of adenovirus in children including dysuria, hematuria, and urinary frequency

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most adenovirus infections are asymptomatic. Adenovirus symptoms include epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, acute hemorrhagic cystitis, and gastroenteritis. Depending on the organ system affected, one may observe fever, pharyngitis, cervical adenopathy, coryza, watery eyes, gross bloody urine, and dyspnea. On physical examination, cervical adenopathy, pharyngoconjunctivitis, and tonsillitis can be seen.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious diseases should be consulted in severely ill patients, especially immunocompromised patients suffering from adenovirus infections. Consultation with an ophthalmologist is essential for the follow-up care of persons with keratoconjunctivitis or corneal opacities. A nephrologist or urologist consultation is considered if hemorrhagic cystitis does not resolve within 5 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Adenoviruses are known as etiologic agents of the gastrointestinal tracts, eye, respiratory, kidney, and other organs (of significance in cases of immunosuppression). Group C adenoviruses are noted to be a critical infective agent of the lower respiratory tract. [3] However, most adenovirus infections are self-limiting, with significant morbidity and death occurring mainly in immunocompromised individuals. Cases are often observed in military recruits, those living close in proximity, and the pediatric population. It is usually transmitted via aerosolized droplets, but the oral-fecal route is also a common transmission mode. Adenovirus infections are common in daycare centers, closed or crowded places such as military barracks, public swimming pools, a household with young children, and medical facilities. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Urethritis -- History and Physical -- Symptoms of Urethritis
retrieved_docs.content: Adenovirus: Adenovirus commonly presents with intense dysuria rather than urethral irritation, aiding in its differentiation from other causes of nongonococcal urethritis. This organism is typically transmitted through oral sex, with upper respiratory tract symptoms occurring generally during the fall and winter months. Patients usually do not report urethral discharge. [2] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchiolitis obliterans Disseminated adenovirus infection Bronchiectasis

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The adenoviruses are DNA viruses common in animals and humans, occurring frequently in adults and children. There are more than 100 serologically different adenovirus types, with 49 types that infect humans. The virus infects multiple organ systems, though most infections are asymptomatic. This activity describes the evaluation and treatment of adenovirus and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the etiology of adenovirus disease. Identify the pathophysiology of adenovirus. Assess the epidemiology of the classic adenovirus disease. Communicate the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance care coordination for patients affected by adenovirus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Adenovirus infection is most common in the early spring or winter but can also occur throughout the year with no distinct seasonality. Susceptibility in children is most often from 6 months to 2 years of age and can occur as well in 5 to 9-year-old children. Infection can also occur from exogenous sources (eg, linens, pillows, lockers, guns), reactivation of the previous virus, and exposure to infected persons by inhalation of aerosolized droplets, conjunctival inoculation, fecal-oral spread. Adenovirus infects military recruits in the United States and other countries as well. Types 3, 4, and 7 leads to pneumonia, acute respiratory diseases in these people. The incubation period lasts from 2 to 14 days. [5] Significantly, latent Adenovirus may exist in renal parenchyma in lymphoid tissue and other tissues for years; in immunocompromised patients, reactivation may occur. Asymptomatic Adenovirus may continue for weeks or months. Though Adenovirus is resistant to many disinfectants, 95% ethanol solution is an active disinfectant. Adenovirus is a widespread cause of upper respiratory tract infections and conjunctivitis. The genome of the Adenovirus within the capsid is highly associated with protein VII--a virus-encoded, histone-like protein. The core protein VII has been associated with the adenovirus genome during the acute phase of infection. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Pyuria -- Evaluation -- Viral Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Viral infections, including herpes simplex virus and HIV, may present with pyuria. Urethritis and cervicitis caused by herpes simplex virus 2 can lead to sterile pyuria; diagnosis is typically based on identifying vesicular lesions during physical examination and confirmed through cell culture or PCR assay. A study on untreated HIV found that 14 of 104 patients (13%) had pyuria. [18] [12] Less frequently encountered viruses, such as cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, BK virus (human polyomavirus 1), and adenovirus, are more common in immunocompromised patients. These viral infections are occasionally associated with pyuria, with hemorrhagic cystitis being the most common presentation.

retrieved_docs.content: Urogenital abnormalities

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Adenovirus infection has an excellent prognosis except in immunocompromised hosts; mortality rates can be up to 70% in immunocompromised individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Gross and Microscopic Hematuria -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In infants and young children, hematuria may signal Wilms tumor, polycystic kidney disease, Alport syndrome, inherited nephritis, glomerulonephritis, hypercalciuria, urinary tract infections, and, in the Black population, sickle cell disease. In older individuals, the most common causes are urinary tract infections, various urological malignancies (eg, kidney and bladder cancers), urolithiasis, postinfectious and other types of glomerulonephritis, trauma, prostatic enlargement, and urinary instrumentation. In about 50% of cases, a specific cause of the hematuria can be identified, meaning no definite etiology can be determined about half the time. When hematuria presents with proteinuria, casts, or dysmorphic (ie, malformed) RBCs, this usually signals glomerular pathology with at least moderate kidney disease. Some common glomerular causes of hematuria include:

retrieved_docs.title: Gross and Microscopic Hematuria -- Evaluation -- Hematuria in Children
retrieved_docs.content: In the pediatric population, gross and microscopic hematuria are relatively common findings with overlapping etiologies. [74] Normal children will have positive urine dipsticks for hematuria 1% to 4% of the time. Unlike adults, hematuria in children rarely occurs due to an underlying malignancy. [75] The family history can suggest a possible underlying hereditary etiology. [31] Asymptomatic, isolated microscopic hematuria is often idiopathic with no identifiable etiology. [74] Microscopic hematuria generally suggests an upper urinary tract source, while gross hematuria is more likely from the bladder or urethra. [31] [75] When a specific cause is identified, the most common diagnoses are glomerulonephritis conditions (eg, poststreptococcal or infectious, IgA nephropathy, and thin basement membrane disease), urinary tract infections, or hypercalciuria. [74] The most common glomerulonephritis in children is poststreptococcal or infectious types. [74] [75] [76] Moreover, proteinuria, fluid overload, casts or dysmorphic RBCs on urinary microscopy, brown "cola" colored hematuria, or hypertension suggests glomerular disease in the pediatric age group, just like in adults. [74] [75]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoviruses -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Adenovirus is diagnosed based on clinical presentation. Other lab work, such as viral culture, PCR, viral antigen assay, and serology, can also help.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2544
retrieved_docs.content: Virology is recognized as an allowable qualifier when evaluating the bladder mass (قيلة مثانية), ureteral obstruction (انسداد الحالب), and rectal mass (قيلة مستقيمية), indicating that viral etiologies or viral testing may be relevant in the diagnostic assessment of these conditions. Conversely, the descriptor “Without” is associated with both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that these lesions can be present in the absence of certain accompanying factors, such as infection. Together, these relationships emphasize that while viral considerations are permissible in the work‑up of these urological and gastrointestinal pathologies, the lesions may also occur independently of other specified conditions.

Genetic and developmental contexts such as genitourinary congenital anomalies, sexually transmitted infections, childhood epilepsy, and Stickler syndrome fall under the congenital, familial, and genetic disorders SMQ, underscoring the overlap of hereditary factors with renal and systemic disease.

retrieved_docs.content: The urinary system is identified as the anatomic site associated with الدَّاءُ السِّيسْتينِي, indicating that this disease primarily involves or impacts the structures of the urinary tract. Recognizing this relationship emphasizes the importance of evaluating urinary system function when diagnosing or managing الدَّاءُ السِّيسْتينِي, as the disease’s manifestations are closely linked to the urinary organs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) is broadly related to urethral discharge (تدلّي الإحليل), indicating that urethral discharge is considered a manifestation or symptom within the wider spectrum of urinary tract disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Urogenital disorders (اضطراب بولي تناسلي) encompass a range of conditions affecting the urinary and reproductive systems, and a urogenital fistula (ناسور بولي تناسلي) is a specific manifestation that falls within this broader category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2893
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital or inherited nature, is applicable to several medical conditions. It can be used to indicate that a blood‑transfusion reaction (تفاعل نقل الدم) may have a congenital component, that infection with an enteric virus (عدوى بفيروس معوي) can be classified as congenital, that inflammation of the bladder, or cystitis (الْتِهابُ المثانة), may arise from congenital factors, and that certain glandular diseases (عضال غدي) are also recognized as potentially congenital. Recognizing these conditions as possibly congenital helps clinicians consider hereditary influences in diagnosis, counseling, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2469
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). These nonspecific genitourinary manifestations frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (kidney stone), indicating that patients presenting with such symptoms should be evaluated for possible calculi within the urinary system. Recognizing that genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions belong to the broader umbrella of urinary tract disorders and are often associated with kidney stones helps clinicians prioritize diagnostic testing and management strategies aimed at identifying and treating underlying stone disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Nephrology Terminology includes a subset of concepts classified as other diseases of the kidney and ureters, indicating that the terminology used in pediatric nephrology specifically encompasses various renal and ureteral disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1550
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases and injuries are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب بولي تناسلي (genitourinary disorder) and are also placed under the broader heading of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many disease and injury entities within the genitourinary system present with nonspecific clinical manifestations. The term Symptoms is described as closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, emphasizing that what clinicians label as symptoms often overlap with the vague or poorly defined signs that accompany various diseases and injuries. This hierarchical relationship highlights the interconnected nature of disease classification, symptom terminology, and the challenge of identifying precise clinical features in genitourinary and other poorly defined conditions, which is important for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1731
retrieved_docs.content: Kidney glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) is a primary renal pathology that can give rise to uremia (اليوريمية) as a downstream clinical manifestation. In addition, the reproductive disorder (اضطراب بولي تناسلي) is classified as a condition that falls under the broader category of kidney glomerular disease, indicating a relationship between glomerular renal pathology and reproductive system involvement. Recognizing that kidney glomerular disease may present with systemic effects such as uremia and may be associated with reproductive abnormalities helps clinicians provide comprehensive assessment and management for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) encompasses a broad range of conditions affecting the urinary system, and within this category lies kidney failure (قصور الكلى). Kidney failure functions as a parent condition for specific renal diseases, including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. Understanding that nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome are subtypes of kidney failure, which itself is a subset of urinary tract disorders, provides clinicians with a hierarchical framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing these interrelated kidney and urinary system conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are noted to co‑occur with حصاة حالبية and also with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that patients who present with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may simultaneously have ureteral stones (حصاة حالبية) or prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing this association can prompt clinicians to consider targeted diagnostic evaluations for these specific urologic pathologies when faced with ambiguous genitourinary presentations, thereby improving detection and management of underlying stone disease or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.
